chunk_summary: Emergency management of symptomatic hypocalcemia in children involves rapid stabilization, IV calcium therapy, and addressing underlying causes like vitamin D deficiency, with close monitoring and etiologic evaluation.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical presentation and emergency management of symptomatic hypocalcemia in children.
Learn the appropriate dosage and administration of intravenous calcium gluconate in pediatric emergencies.
Identify key laboratory investigations to determine the etiology of hypocalcemia.
Develop a monitoring and follow-up plan for patients with hypocalcemia.
key_facts: Symptomatic hypocalcemia can present with seizures, tetany, stridor, or hypocalcemic cardiomyopathy.
Initial treatment is 10% calcium gluconate at 1–2 mL/kg per dose IV, administered slowly under cardiac monitoring.
Monitor for bradycardia, arrhythmias, and prolonged QT interval during IV calcium administration.
Etiologic evaluation includes serum calcium, phosphate, magnesium, alkaline phosphatase, creatinine, albumin, PTH, and 25(OH)D levels.
Transition to oral calcium and vitamin D therapy after stabilization, with repeat serum calcium checks within 24–48 hours.
reference_quote: Immediate treatment is intravenous calcium. A widely used pediatric anchor is 10% calcium gluconate 1–2 mL/kg per dose given slowly by IV route under cardiac monitoring.
